pediatricBMI.name: Pediatric BMI Calculator
pediatricBMI.description: Calculate BMI percentile for children and teens (2–20 years) — age- and sex-specific per WHO references.

meta.title: Pediatric BMI Calculator — BMI Percentile for Children & Teens
meta.description: Calculate your child's BMI (ages 2–20) and the age- and sex-specific percentile per WHO references. Identify underweight, healthy weight, overweight, and obesity. Free, no signup.

pediatricBMI.title: Pediatric BMI Calculator
pediatricBMI.description: BMI for children and teens is interpreted as an age- and sex-specific percentile, not against fixed adult cut-offs.
pediatricBMI.advice_underweight: BMI is below the 5th percentile. A medical evaluation is recommended to rule out causes such as undernutrition, growth disorders, or chronic conditions.
pediatricBMI.advice_healthy: BMI is in the healthy range (5th to below 85th percentile). Maintain balanced nutrition, regular activity, and adequate sleep.
pediatricBMI.advice_overweight: BMI is in the overweight range (85th to below 95th percentile). A consultation with the pediatrician about nutrition, activity, and screen time is advisable.
pediatricBMI.advice_obesity: BMI is in the obesity range (≥ 95th percentile). A medical assessment with targeted lifestyle or treatment measures is recommended.
pediatricBMI.howItWorksText: In children and teens, body composition changes with age — a single BMI number only makes sense relative to peers of the same age and sex. This calculator uses the WHO BMI-for-age reference tables (LMS method with Box-Cox transformation) and reports the percentile. CDC weight-status categories: < 5th = underweight, 5th–85th = healthy, 85th–95th = overweight, ≥ 95th = obesity.
pediatricBMI.disclaimer: This calculator is not a substitute for medical advice. Growth and BMI vary individually — discuss any concerns with your pediatrician.

faq.q: Why are children's BMI cut-offs different from adults'?
faq.a: Body fat percentage changes substantially during growth — a BMI of 20 is high for a 5-year-old and low for a teenager. So pediatric BMI is interpreted as an age- and sex-specific percentile, not via fixed cut-offs.
75% of same-age, same-sex peers have a lower BMI; 25% have a higher one. Values between the 5th and 85th percentile are considered healthy.

faq.q: At what percentile is a child considered overweight?
faq.a: Per CDC and WHO: 85th to below 95th percentile = overweight, ≥ 95th percentile = obesity. Below the 5th percentile is classed as underweight.

faq.q: What ages does this calculator cover?
faq.a: From age 2 through 20 (24 to 240 months). For infants under 2, weight-for-length percentiles are used instead of BMI.

faq.q: What method is used to compute the percentile?
faq.a: The WHO LMS method with a Box-Cox transformation: age- and sex-specific L, M, and S parameters are used to compute a z-score, then converted via the standard normal distribution to a percentile.

faq.q: My child is tall and heavy — does that mean overweight?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Muscle mass and body build influence BMI. Athletic or late-maturing children may have a high or low BMI without clinical concern. When in doubt, get a clinical assessment.

faq.q: How often should I check my child's BMI?
faq.a: Pediatric BMI is recorded at routine well-child visits. Additional checks every 6–12 months are generally sufficient unless concerns arise.
